instruction.global: What would happen to the two bullets after the hydraulic press fully descends
steps: Hydraulic ram descends, contacting bullets and initiating vertical compression and buckling
Continued downward force causes severe plastic deformation and horizontal expansion of casings
Ram fully compresses bullets, flattening them completely against the base

explain: Compressive stress exceeds the material's yield strength, causing plastic deformation
Material ductility permits extensive plastic flow without immediate shattering
Extreme pressure forces lateral material displacement, overcoming structural integrity
invariants: Camera angle and field of view

instruction.global: What would happen to the slime as hands pull it apart in opposite directions
steps: Hands grip the cubic block and pull outwards, deforming it into a rectangular shape
Hands move further apart, elongating the material which thins in the center
Continued pulling stretches the material into a long, thin strand showing internal texture

explain: Applied tensile stress causes initial viscoelastic deformation of the material
Conservation of volume necessitates cross-sectional thinning as the object elongates
Viscoelastic properties allow significant strain and polymer alignment without immediate rupture
invariants: The two hands manipulating the object
Composition of the material (slime with beads)

instruction.global: What would happen to the modeling clay in hand as the thumb presses down forcefully
steps: Thumbs press into the pink sphere, initiating surface cracks while view zooms in
Thumbs push outward, widening the crack and revealing the granular internal texture
The sphere is torn completely open, fully exposing the crumbly interior structure

explain: Applied stress exceeds the material's yield strength, causing fracture initiation
Tensile forces drive crack propagation, overcoming internal cohesive bonds
Continued work against material cohesion results in complete macroscopic separation
invariants: Hands with blue floral nail art

instruction.global: What would happen to the metal cans after being run over by the car tire
steps: Tire rolls left, crushing the pink and red boxes while the others remain intact.
Tire advances further, flattening the white box and compressing the blue box.
Tire clears the frame, leaving all four boxes permanently flattened on the asphalt.

explain: Compressive stress exceeds the material's yield strength, causing plastic deformation.
Rolling motion transfers the vehicle's weight, applying vertical crushing force to subsequent objects.
Plasticity ensures the metal retains its deformed shape after the external load is removed.

instruction.global: What would happen to the bucket with alcohol-coated interior after being ignited and having its opening sealed by hand
steps: Hand seals bottle opening; upper walls begin to buckle inward
Bottle body collapses rapidly inward, drastically reducing internal volume
Bottle reaches fully crumpled state and deformation stabilizes

explain: Sealing traps hot gas; rapid cooling lowers internal pressure (Gay-Lussac's Law)
External atmospheric pressure exceeds internal pressure, creating unbalanced inward force
Volume reduction increases internal pressure until equilibrium is restored (Boyle's Law)

instruction.global: What would happen to the gold after the hydraulic press continues applying downward pressure
steps: The press descends, compressing the cylinder which buckles and bulges outward.
The press moves lower, flattening the object significantly and forcing it wider.
The press nearly touches the base, squashing the object into a thin sheet.

explain: Compressive stress exceeds yield strength, causing plastic deformation and buckling.
Plastic flow causes lateral expansion to conserve volume under vertical compression.
Continued compressive force maximizes deformation against the rigid surface.

instruction.global: What would happen to the plastic water bottle after the hydraulic press continues applying downward pressure
steps: Hydraulic ram descends, contacts the bottle, and initiates compression
Ram presses further, causing severe flattening and plastic deformation
Full compression occurs; internal air pressure violently ejects the cap

explain: Hydraulic force overcomes the bottle's initial structural rigidity
Compressive stress exceeds material yield strength causing permanent deformation
Rapid reduction of volume increases air pressure, launching the cap

instruction.global: What would happen to the toilet paper roll after the hydraulic press continues applying downward pressure
steps: Piston descends, compressing the white roll vertically; sides buckle and wrinkle significantly.
Piston lowers further, crushing the roll into a compact, flattened, irregular shape.
Piston reaches bottom, fully flattening the material into a thin, solid disk.

explain: Compressive stress exceeds critical load, causing structural buckling and plastic deformation.
Continuous external force maintains plastic flow, reducing volume and rearranging material.
Extreme pressure causes maximum material compaction and lateral expansion (Poisson effect).

instruction.global: What would happen to the thermos cup after the hydraulic press fully descends
steps: The press plate descends, causing the top of the flask to buckle and wrinkle.
Continued downward pressure vertically shortens the flask, forming deep accordion-like folds.
The flask is completely flattened into a compact, compressed metal puck.

explain: Compressive stress exceeds the cylinder's critical buckling load, initiating structural instability.
Plastic deformation allows the metal to yield and fold progressively under sustained force.
Extreme compressive strain forces complete structural collapse and material densification.

instruction.global: What would happen to the aluminum ball after the hydraulic press fully descends
steps: The upper press plate descends, making contact and slightly compressing the foil ball
The plate pushes down further, plastically deforming the ball into a flattened puck
The plate descends to its lowest point, crushing the foil into a thin disk

explain: Hydraulic force application initiates compressive stress on the structure
Stress exceeds the material's yield strength, causing irreversible plastic deformation
Continued work results in maximum material densification and lateral expansion
invariants: Yellow and black hazard pattern on the press

instruction.global: What would happen to the modeling clay after the hydraulic press fully descends
steps: Ram descends, compressing the stack; clay shortens vertically and expands laterally.
Continued compression flattens the clay layers, forcing them to spread wider.
Ram nearly touches base, crushing clay into a thin, uniform sheet.

explain: Compressive stress causes plastic deformation; conservation of volume forces lateral expansion.
Sustained force exceeds yield strength, driving continuous plastic flow of material.
Extreme pressure forces maximum lateral displacement of the incompressible solid mass.

instruction.global: What would happen to the aluminum block after the hydraulic press fully descends
steps: The upper ram descends, closing the gap to contact the aluminum cube
The cube is compressed vertically and expands horizontally, losing its cubic shape
The cube is flattened completely into a thin disk structure

explain: Hydraulic force drives the linear motion of the ram (Newton's Second Law)
Compressive stress exceeds the material's yield strength, causing plastic deformation
High ductility allows the metal to flow plastically without fracturing
invariants: Yellow and black hazard stripes on the press

instruction.global: What would happen to the iron rod after the hammer fully strikes down
steps: Hammer descends to contact rod, initiating downward bending
Hammer continues downward, forcing rod into a V-shape
Hammer presses deeper, sharpening the final bend angle

explain: Applied force creates bending moment exceeding material yield strength
Plastic deformation occurs as stress exceeds the yield point
Work done by force causes further permanent structural strain

instruction.global: What would happen to the dough after the rolling pin is pressed back and forth with force
steps: Hands press the rolling pin downward, compressing the initial clay lump
Rolling pin moves forward, flattening the clay and extending its shape
Rolling motion completes, leaving a thin, expanded sheet of clay

explain: Downward force creates compressive stress causing initial plastic deformation
Continuous pressure and rolling motion induce material flow and elongation
Conservation of volume necessitates surface area expansion as thickness decreases

instruction.global: What would happen to this apple when the hydraulic press fully descends
steps: Hydraulic ram descends, contacts apple, and initiates vertical compression
Continued compression causes lateral expansion, skin rupture, and juice release
Apple is completely flattened and crushed into pulp

explain: Compressive stress causes elastic and initial plastic deformation
Internal pressure exceeds the material's ultimate tensile strength
Extreme strain causes total structural collapse and fracture

instruction.global: What would happen to this iron pillar after the hydraulic press partially descends and then lifts
steps: Load increases to ~81t; cylinder compresses axially and begins to deform
Load stabilizes around ~81t; cylinder buckles laterally to the right
Load rises to ~99t; cylinder collapses into a severe plastic bend

explain: Compressive stress exceeds the material's yield strength, initiating plasticity
Structural instability (buckling) occurs as the column exceeds critical load
Strain hardening increases material resistance, requiring higher force to deform

instruction.global: What would happen to these three beverage bottles after a car wheel rolls over them
steps: Tire rolls forward, contacting the cans and initiating structural buckling
Tire fully compresses cans, causing collapse and violent liquid spray
Tire rolls past the objects, leaving permanently flattened cans and foam

explain: Compressive force from the tire exceeds the aluminum's yield strength
Rapid volume reduction spikes internal fluid pressure causing explosive rupture
Irreversible plastic deformation prevents the metal from recovering its shape

instruction.global: What would happen to this copper pillar after the hydraulic press partially descends and then lifts
steps: The press descends, causing the copper cylinder to shorten vertically and bulge outward.
The cylinder undergoes further compression, becoming slightly shorter and wider.
The cylinder is flattened into a short, squat shape; a gloved hand enters the frame.

explain: Compressive stress exceeds yield strength, causing plastic deformation and lateral expansion (barreling).
Continued plastic flow maintains constant volume by increasing cross-sectional area as height decreases.
Accumulated strain under high pressure results in significant permanent geometric deformation.

instruction.global: What would happen to this watermelon-shaped clay when pressed firmly by a hand
steps: Hands apply outward tension, causing the green rind to crack and reveal pink foam.
The fissure widens significantly as pulling continues, exposing the porous internal structure.
The object is torn completely into two separate halves with jagged, textured surfaces.

explain: Applied tensile stress exceeds the material's yield strength, initiating fracture.
Continued external work propagates the crack through the material matrix.
Sustained tension causes complete structural failure and separation.

instruction.global: What would happen to this glowing red-hot nickel ball after the hydraulic press fully descends and remains for a long time
steps: Hydraulic press descends, contacting the sphere and causing initial flattening.
Continued compression flattens the sphere into a wider, red-hot disc shape.
Object flattens further and turns dark grey as it loses incandescence.

explain: Compressive stress exceeds the material's yield strength, initiating plastic deformation.
Continuous plastic flow spreads material laterally under sustained vertical force.
Rapid conductive heat transfer to the cold press plates causes quenching.
